questions.scenario: You need to store a list of reactor services in a variable without relying on string splitting.
questions.question: Create an indexed array SERVICES with values 'nginx', 'postgres', 'monitor' and print all elements.
questions.description: Arrays in bash: arr=(elem1 elem2 elem3). Access all elements with ${arr[@]}. Access one with ${arr[0]}. Arrays preserve multi-word elements correctly — unlike space-separated strings, they don't break on spaces.
use_cases: Store a list of servers without delimiter confusion
Pass multiple values to a function cleanly
Collect command outputs in a loop

questions.scenario: You need to access specific elements of an array by index.
questions.question: Create an array of three items and print the second element (index 1).
questions.description: Arrays are 0-indexed. ${arr[0]} is the first element, ${arr[1]} is second. ${arr[-1]} is the last. Bash arrays are sparse — you can have indices 0, 5, 100 with nothing in between.
use_cases: Access specific positional items in a list
Process the first and last element differently

questions.scenario: You need to iterate over all services in an array and print a status line for each.
questions.question: Loop over SERVICES=(nginx postgres monitor) and print 'Checking: SERVICE' for each.
questions.description: for item in "${arr[@]}"; do ... done iterates over array elements. The quotes around "${arr[@]}" are critical — without them, elements with spaces split into separate words.
use_cases: Apply an operation to each server in a list
Health-check all services in a config array
Validate each item in a list
hints: Use for item in "${ARRAY[@]}" — note the quotes
Run: SERVICES=(nginx postgres monitor) && for s in "${SERVICES[@]}"; do echo "Checking: $s"; done

validation.command: SERVICES=(nginx postgres monitor) && for s in "${SERVICES[@]}"; do echo "Checking: $s"; done

questions.scenario: A script must know how many services are in the list before deciding whether to alert.
questions.question: Print the number of elements in SERVICES=(nginx postgres monitor backup).
questions.description: ${#arr[@]} returns the number of elements in the array. ${#arr[0]} returns the length of the first element string. The @ and # combination is specific to array length.
use_cases: Alert if too many services are registered
Validate array is non-empty before iterating
Compute loop bounds from array size
hints: Run: SERVICES=(nginx postgres monitor backup) && echo ${#SERVICES[@]}

questions.scenario: You need to map service names to their ports for a config generator.
questions.question: Create an associative array mapping nginx→80 and postgres→5432. Print the postgres port.
questions.description: Associative arrays use string keys: declare -A arr; arr[key]=value. Access: ${arr[key]}. Keys can be any string. Must declare with -A before use — unlike indexed arrays. Bash 4+ only.
use_cases: Map service names to ports or IPs
Store key-value config without external files
hints: declare -A first, then assign with arr[key]=val
Run: declare -A PORTS && PORTS[nginx]=80 && PORTS[postgres]=5432 && echo ${PORTS[postgres]}
